title: E-06/94
text:
ORDER OF THE COURT
25 November 1994
(Procedure – Admissibility)
In Case E-6/94
Reinhard Helmers, residing in Lund (Sweden), not represented by a lawyer and
with no address for service in Geneva,
applicant,
v
the EFTA Surveillance Authority,
and
the Kingdom of Sweden
defendant,
APPLICATION for an order requiring the EFTA Surveillance Authority to take a
decision concerning an alleged infringement by the Government of Sweden of the
EEA Agreement,
THE COURT
composed of: Leif Sevón, President (Rapporteur), Bjørn Haug and Kurt Herndl
Judges,
Registrar: Heikki Kanninen, Assistant Registrar,

makes the following
ORDER
1 On 13 June 1994 the applicant lodged, without being represented by a lawyer, an
application alleging an infringement of the EEA Agreement by the Government of
Sweden. This application was subsequently refined by several letters to the Court
to be an action also against the EFTA Surveillance Authority. In the light of these
refinements the applicant claims that the Court should:
(1) Order the Government of Sweden to
(a) end its discrimination against the applicant,
(b) re-install the applicant in his original position at Lund University,
(c) pay the applicant accumulated compensation in accordance with a
collective contract,
(d) pay the appropriate social insurance and pension contributions, and
(e) pay interest in accordance with the Swedish law on interest;
(2) Declare that the EFTA Surveillance Authority has infringed Article 37,
second and third paragraphs, of the Agreement between the EFTA States on
the Establishment of a Surveillance Authority and a Court of Justice by
failing to decide on the applicant´s complaint within two months of it being
lodged with the Authority;
(3) Declare void the decision of the EFTA Surveillance Authority of 20 July
1994 to close the applicant´s case against the Government of Sweden
concerning unjustified discrimination as being outside the scope of the
Authority's duty of surveillance, in accordance with Article 36, fourth
paragraph, of the Agreement between the EFTA States on the
Establishment of a Surveillance Authority and a Court of Justice.
2 By letter of 26 August 1994 the Registrar of the Court informed the applicant of
the content of Article 17, second paragraph, of the Statute of the EFTA Court and
of Articles 32(1), 33 and 45 of the Rules of Procedure of the EFTA Court and
instructed him, inter alia, to be represented by a lawyer.
3 In a letter to the Court dated 31 August 1994 the applicant expressed the view that
the restrictions in Article 17 of the Statute of the EFTA Court did not apply, since
2

he, although not a lawyer himself, has all the rights of a lawyer "entitled to
practise before a court" in Sweden.
4 In a letter dated 20 September 1994, by which the Registrar of the Court informed
the applicant of the formal requirements of applications to the Court, the applicant
was reminded, with reference to the articles mentioned, that a private person who
is party to the proceedings before the EFTA Court must be represented by a
lawyer entitled to practise before a court in a Contracting Party to the EEA
Agreement, and that the original of every pleading must be signed by that party's
lawyer. The applicant was requested to put the application in order by 11 October
1994 at the latest.
5 In a letter dated 29 September 1994 the applicant requested the permission of the
Court to present his case in person before the Court.
6 The Registrar of the Court reminded the applicant in a letter dated 5 October 1994
that the Court is obliged to comply with the Statute and the Rules of Procedure of
the EFTA Court, and that the time-limit remained unchanged.
7 In a note transmitted to the Court by a telefax dated 10 October 1994 and received
by the Court on that day the applicant informed the Court that he would need an
extension of at least a fortnight for the submission of additional documents to his
application. No original of this note as required by Article 32(6) of the Rules of
Procedure was subsequently received by the Court.
8 After the expiry of the time-limit mentioned the applicant sent the Court two
letters, signed only by himself, of which one appears to be intended as a
supplement to the application.
9 Article 88(1) of the Rules of Procedure provides that the Court, where an action is
manifestly inadmissible, may by reasoned order, and without taking further steps
in the proceedings, give a decision on the action.
10 According to the provisions of Article 17, second paragraph, of the Statute of the
EFTA Court and Article 32(1) of the Rules of Procedure an individual must be
represented before the Court by a lawyer. This lawyer must be entitled to practise
before a court in a Contracting Party to the EEA Agreement. The lawyer must sign
the original of every pleading. The Court has no power to grant a derogation from
these provisions. The fact that a party may present his case in person in national
courts of some of the Contracting EFTA States does not affect the prescribed
requirement that a party bringing a case before the EFTA Court must be
represented by a lawyer. The applicant was informed of the content of the
provisions and given time to comply with these formal legal requirements.
3

11 As the applicant has not availed himself of the opportunity to put his application in
order, it must be declared inadmissible and removed from the Court's Register.
12 Under these circumstances the Court need not express itself on other formal
aspects of the application.
On these grounds,
THE COURT
hereby orders:
1. The application is dismissed as inadmissible,
2. The case shall be removed from the Register of the Court.
Leif Sevón Bjørn Haug Kurt Herndl
Delivered in Geneva, 25 November 1994
Heikki Kanninen Leif Sevón
Assistant Registrar President
4
